refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed absolute.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, congestion profiles) agreed with customer. Measure across multiple teleoperation sessions and load scenarios. Acceptance: sustained packet loss ≤1% (or customer-defined target) over a minimum observation window (e.g., 1 hour continuous operation).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall provide network condition monitoring and graceful degradation when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services subsystem shall enforce operational constraints when teleoperation communication quality falls below safety-critical thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive bitrate encoding and forward error correction (FEC) in the teleoperation link to reduce effective packet loss impact on command/feedback streams.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication paths (dual network links with automatic failover) to achieve statistical packet loss reduction through path diversity.
DP-C: Implement application-layer acknowledgment and selective retransmission of critical teleoperation commands and sensor feedback, with timeout-based recovery.
DP-D: Combine DP-A (FEC), DP-C (selective retransmission), and network monitoring to achieve target packet loss performance through layered mitigation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layer packet loss mitigation strategy combining forward error correction (FEC) at the transport layer, application-layer selective retransmission for critical teleoperation commands and safety-critical sensor feedback, and continuous network condition monitoring with operator alerts and graceful degradation triggers.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation subsystem (FEC, retransmission logic) with safety services (degradation triggers, operator alerts). This is acceptable because safety criticality demands tight integration; however, the coupling must be managed via well-defined interfaces (SafetyAlert, Tasking).

DP-D couples network link quality monitoring with teleoperation command/feedback processing. This is a functional dependency, not a design flaw, and is mitigated by clear separation of concerns (monitoring vs. processing).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is not orthogonal but is justified by safety criticality (high). The multi-layer approach reduces risk of single-point failure in packet loss mitigation. Coupling is managed through explicit interface contracts (SafetyAlert for safety state transitions, Tasking for command flow control).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment. Risk: If actual network conditions differ significantly, packet loss target may not be achievable without additional infrastructure investment.
Assumption: FEC overhead (bandwidth increase) is acceptable within the teleoperation link capacity. Risk: If bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may degrade performance or require link upgrade.
Assumption: Selective retransmission logic can distinguish critical (safety-relevant) from non-critical teleoperation data. Risk: Misclassification could compromise safety or operator effectiveness.

Assumption: Operator alerts and graceful degradation are acceptable operational modes (e.g., reduced speed, restricted functionality). Risk: If customer requires full functionality under all conditions, DP-D may be insufficient.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define: (1) FEC algorithm and overhead budget, (2) classification criteria for critical vs. non-critical teleoperation data, (3) retransmission timeout and retry limits, (4) network condition thresholds triggering graceful degradation, and (5) operator notification and manual override procedures. These details should be captured in sub-level DPs during decomposition.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication pipeline, including command encoding/transmission, sensor feedback reception/decoding, FEC implementation, selective retransmission logic, and network condition monitoring. It interfaces with Safety Services for degradation triggers and with the Vehicle for command execution and telemetry collection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (e.g., movement, tool control) from tele-operator or autonomous planner. Tasking is the input stream that Teleoperation encodes, applies FEC, and transmits to the Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs decoded sensor feedback (position, orientation, load, environmental data) from the Vehicle to the tele-operator or autonomous system. Telemetry is the output stream that Teleoperation receives, applies error correction, and delivers to consumers.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals Safety Services when network packet loss exceeds safety-critical thresholds, triggering graceful degradation (e.g., speed limits, operational constraints). SafetyAlert is the control signal that Teleoperation emits to enforce safety-critical state transitions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Represents the underlying communication medium (e.g., cellular, satellite, WiFi, wired). Teleoperation monitors Network Link quality (packet loss, latency, jitter) and adapts FEC/retransmission parameters accordingly.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. FEC, retransmission, and network monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services (a necessary safety coupling, not diffusion).

Safety Services should NOT own packet loss mitigation (FEC, retransmission). However, Safety Services must own the policy for graceful degradation and operational constraints when communication quality degrades. This is a safety policy concern, not a communication engineering concern. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would diffuse communication logic across modules.

Vehicle should NOT own teleoperation packet loss mitigation. Vehicle owns command execution and telemetry generation, not communication quality management. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would couple vehicle control logic with network engineering, violating separation of concerns.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own DP-D. Assigning to NGHE Solution would defer the architectural decision and diffuse responsibility across multiple sub-modules. Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the correct architectural owner for DP-D (multi-layer packet loss mitigation). Teleoperation owns the communication pipeline and has direct visibility into packet loss, FEC overhead, retransmission state, and network conditions. Safety Services owns the policy for graceful degradation (via SafetyAlert interface), but Teleoperation owns the mechanism. This separation preserves modularity: communication engineering (Teleoperation) is decoupled from safety policy (Safety Services).
containment_risks: Risk: If Teleoperation module is not yet decomposed into sub-modules (FEC layer, retransmission layer, monitoring layer), DP-D may be too coarse-grained for a single module. Mitigation: Proceed with assignment to Teleoperation at this level; decompose Teleoperation in the next level to allocate FEC, retransmission, and monitoring to sub-modules.

Risk: If Teleoperation module does not have direct access to Network Link quality metrics, it cannot implement network condition monitoring. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface provides packet loss, latency, and jitter telemetry to Teleoperation.

Risk: If Safety Services does not receive timely SafetyAlert signals from Teleoperation, graceful degradation may be delayed, compromising safety. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert as a high-priority, low-latency interface with strict timing requirements.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that the Teleoperation module has the following capabilities before proceeding: (1) access to Network Link quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter), (2) ability to encode/decode FEC, (3) state machine for selective retransmission, (4) interface to Safety Services for SafetyAlert signaling. If any capability is missing, revise the module boundary or add sub-modules to Teleoperation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layer packet loss mitigation) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (teleoperation packet loss ≤1%). DP-D combines FEC, selective retransmission, and network monitoring—three complementary mechanisms that together achieve the packet loss target. The DP is traceable to the FR and is not over-constrained or under-specified at this level.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk (Low): SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services. This is a necessary safety coupling, not diffusion, because packet loss is a safety-critical condition. However, ensure that SafetyAlert is a clean, well-defined interface (not a back-channel for other concerns).

Diffusion Risk (Low): Network Link interface couples Teleoperation to the underlying communication medium. This is expected and necessary; Teleoperation must monitor network quality to adapt FEC/retransmission parameters.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (95/100) for DP-D. FEC, retransmission, and network monitoring are core teleoperation concerns and should not be diffused to Vehicle, Safety Services, or NGHE Solution. The Teleoperation module is the correct architectural owner. Proceed with assignment.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via end-to-end packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module must expose packet loss metrics (e.g., via Telemetry interface or a dedicated monitoring interface) for verification. Acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss) is measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Teleoperation owns the mechanisms (FEC, retransmission, monitoring) that achieve the packet loss target. Safety Services owns the policy for graceful degradation (via SafetyAlert). The allocation is unambiguous and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify FEC algorithm (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes), overhead budget, and performance characteristics.
Define retransmission timeout, retry limits, and backoff strategy to avoid congestion collapse.
Specify network condition thresholds (e.g., packet loss >2%, latency >500ms) that trigger SafetyAlert and graceful degradation.
Ensure Teleoperation module has access to Network Link quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) via a well-defined interface.
Confirm that Safety Services receives SafetyAlert signals with low latency (<100ms) to enable timely graceful degradation.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies four sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, network monitoring, safety enforcement) that must be decomposed into lower-level FRs, DPs, and IMs. DP-D is a sound multi-layer strategy but is too coarse-grained for a single module without further refinement. Teleoperation module assignment is correct and well-justified, but the next level must decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (FEC layer, retransmission layer, monitoring layer) and allocate sub-DPs to each. Proceed with decomposition of sub-FRs.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR explicitly identifies four sub-FRs that are not yet addressed by a single DP. DP-D is a composite strategy that combines three mechanisms (FEC, retransmission, monitoring). To ensure each mechanism is properly designed and verified, each sub-FR must be decomposed into its own DP and allocated to a sub-module of Teleoperation. This is a standard hierarchical decomposition pattern: the parent level (FR-NGHE-009 -> DP-D -> Teleoperation) establishes the overall strategy, and the child level decomposes the strategy into implementable sub-modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting. (Decompose to: DP-009.1 = Implement packet loss counter and statistics collector; IM-009.1 = Teleoperation.NetworkMonitor sub-module)

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity. (Decompose to: DP-009.2a = Implement FEC encoder/decoder; IM-009.2a = Teleoperation.FECLayer sub-module; DP-009.2b = Implement selective retransmission with timeout/retry; IM-009.2b = Teleoperation.RetransmissionLayer sub-module)

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall provide network condition monitoring and graceful degradation when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds. (Decompose to: DP-009.3 = Implement network condition state machine and degradation trigger logic; IM-009.3 = Teleoperation.NetworkMonitor sub-module with SafetyAlert signaling)

FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services subsystem shall enforce operational constraints when teleoperation communication quality falls below safety-critical thresholds. (Decompose to: DP-009.4 = Implement safety policy enforcement (speed limits, operational constraints) based on SafetyAlert signals; IM-009.4 = Safety Services.DegradationPolicy sub-module)

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment.
FEC overhead (bandwidth increase) is acceptable within the teleoperation link capacity.
Selective retransmission logic can reliably distinguish critical (safety-relevant) from non-critical teleoperation data.
Operator alerts and graceful degradation (e.g., reduced speed, restricted functionality) are acceptable operational modes.
Teleoperation module has access to Network Link quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter).
Safety Services receives SafetyAlert signals with low latency (<100ms).
Packet loss target (≤1%) is achievable with the proposed multi-layer mitigation strategy under agreed network conditions.
open_risks: Risk: If actual network conditions differ significantly from agreed conditions, packet loss target may not be achievable without additional infrastructure investment.
Risk: If FEC overhead is too high, it may degrade overall teleoperation performance or require link upgrade.
Risk: If retransmission timeout is too short, it may cause congestion collapse; if too long, it may increase latency unacceptably.
Risk: If SafetyAlert latency exceeds safety-critical thresholds, graceful degradation may be delayed, compromising safety.

Risk: If Teleoperation module is not properly decomposed into sub-modules (FEC, retransmission, monitoring), implementation may diffuse logic across multiple code files or components, violating modularity.
evidence_needed: Network condition profile (latency, jitter, packet loss distribution) under agreed deployment scenarios.
FEC algorithm performance data (overhead, latency, error correction capability) for candidate algorithms (Reed-Solomon, Turbo, LDPC).
Retransmission timeout and retry strategy analysis (e.g., exponential backoff, congestion avoidance).
Safety-critical threshold definition (e.g., packet loss >2%, latency >500ms) and justification.
Teleoperation module interface specification (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) with timing and reliability requirements.
Verification plan for packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions (test scenarios, acceptance criteria, measurement methodology).
Graceful degradation policy specification (e.g., speed limits, operational constraints) and operator notification procedures.
